speaker: interviewer
text: I have an interview with Walter Hartmann. Excuse me. After the war, did you find out what happened to your relatives in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not necessarily in detail, but in general, yes. My father's family, his younger-- his brother, the wife, the son, were lost to the Nazis. The only ones alive from my father's family is my brother, I, and the cousin we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, who?

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the ones who died in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hartmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hotman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hartmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The maiden ladies who were his sisters all died, all-- all were murdered. In my mother's case, we were a little more fortunate. We lost to the Nazis one family, family Jonas. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her eldest brother committed suicide, I'm sorry to say. By the way, if you happen to have ever heard, or somebody has heard of a rather well-known and fairly important German writer by the name Stefan Heym, his original name was Helmut Frei. He's my first cousin. He made it, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me see. Who else did not?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Rosenberg-- do you know, I think most of the others made it, one way or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you're in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- did you-- did you date? Did you meet anybody? What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I mentioned to you before, I had to [? go out ?] pretty fast if I wanted to deal with the British government, which I did, which made my discovery of girls a bit-- how should we put it-- a little slow. But again, I was enormously fortunate and met one or two young ladies who were very sweet to me, and very nice to me, and put up with me for quite awhile. One of them, I had difficulty getting rid of. She was really quite keen. Why, I don't know. Fortunately, I went to another school. I was a schoolteacher then in England, during the war. Went to another school. And there I happened to be running around with some Land girls. These were-- women were called up for war work in World War Two. And some women were volunteered for the Land Army, which grew food for people, a very important thing. Well, two of them ran our school, which had large grounds. And she-- and they had boarders and kids who ate there. And they produced vegetables. And I happened to know these-- or see these young ladies. Well, when we had what you might call-- well, we did call sports days, it was a big, formal kind of thing. And I noticed a young lady over there in civvies, not in a Land Army uniform, who really looked quite appetizing. And that was one of our Land girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I suggested that we go to the movie together, if she'll bring her sister. I was-- had been burnt by the love of girls. So I wanted to be on the safe side. So my wife, by the way, tells this story differently. But this is the-- this is the true version. So we went to the movies. And we went to the movies again. And to cut a long story short, we were a little bit of a couple. Well, you know, having a nice time together.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no way in which we could have possibly think of marrying. She came from a well-established family, you know, Catholic, Protestant, all kinds of things. And here I was, a penniless European Jew, with no-- I mean, this was nonsense, you see. However, the young lady didn't seem to know much about nonsense. One of the things was she had been convincingly-- convincedly religious. But I had trouble. I got in trouble with her-- all right, Church of England is the word in England. In America, we're Episcopalian, which is close to Roman Catholic. And it didn't work for her. And she said I was the first Jew she'd ever met. I don't know when she-- how I surprised her or whether I surprised her. And she asked me questions. And I answered them as well as I could. Then she started upsetting me a great deal. She was thinking of learning more about Judaism and perhaps becoming Jewish. And I said, don't do that. Ask-- talk of-- talk to people, and so and so. Well, to cut a very long story short, the lady insisted. And when I was about to go to America, I felt that I couldn't go without having a firm arrangement with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bought a ring, about worth maybe half a dollar or a dollar-- it was silver, though. And because all my money was already in America, and you know, cheap-- and asked her to marry me. And she said yes. And that's my wife, I'm afraid. She-- I don't know, she says she did all right. But I don't think she got the better of the bargain. However this may be, if the good Lord is kind, what is today, the 9th, you say? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in a month and two weeks, we have been married 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had three children who, of course, are obviously the best, brightest, most beautiful ever in-- you've seen, of course. Two girls, one boy. The two girls are married with three and two grandsons, respectively.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me their names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandsons?

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the children, the oldest one is Gail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second one is Julie. The boy is Alexander. The grandsons are, Gail's; Joshua, Benjamin, and Aaron. If you insist on their middle names, I can probably recall them. Julie's two sons are Jeremy and Ethan. And once again, they are, of course, the last [? word in ?] grandchildren. As a matter of fact, they are rather remarkably--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the circumstances under which you came to the United States, and what your eventual profession became.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, OK. You see, at the end of World War II, I found that I had, really, an option of four rather nice-looking nationalities. I could have stayed in England and become English. I could've gone to Germany. The Germans were very keen on having us, and-- and did a lot for us. I could have gone to Israel. But today, I'm pretty sure, it was a Haganah agent rather, tried to persuade me to it. Or I could have gone to the United States. As you can see, I chose United States. England, I think, was great.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my feeling was for at least three generations, you could get guest privileges in this club, but you couldn't become a member, not really. Germany? Don't be funny. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Israel was exciting and interesting, and certainly worth thinking about. However, it seemed to me that the United States had two or three things. One, they were a multi-- we today say multicultural. In those days, we used different words, society.

speaker: interviewer
text: History would be made from the United States or not at all. And of course, there was the-- what is it? The American dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not only are the-- are the streets being paved with gold, but other dreams as well. The land of opportunity. I was aware of the fact then, before I came, that it could easily become the land of the wasted opportunity. But we decided to come to the United States and have not regretted it. I did some school teaching. I went somewhere other than-- it's a long, long and complicated story, But I somehow or other diddled the University of Chicago into giving me a few degrees and ended up as a psychology-- first, other things, a research associate, and religious education and things like that. But I ended up as an instructor in psychology and-- and Professor Emeritus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that what you wanted to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's exactly what I wanted to know. Is there anything else, Walter, that-- that you want to cover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear. You're asking me what else do I want to cover in my life? There are many, many, many things, but not, I think, any that are really terribly relevant to what we are trying to do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I have saved my parents? I think I could have, if I had been a little more patient. Why did I become a psychologist? You see, I witnessed, in my own case, but in other cases too, a collapse of a-- of a very firm-looking and firm-feeling world. I mean, a complete-- you know, I mean, how can this happen? How can this be?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it occurred to me that maybe philosophy would help me. But I came to the conclusion, probably not. And so I went into psychology, all to maybe help me explain, make some sense out of what had happened to me and to others. And so I, in a way, I suppose, became a psychologist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I feel [? dead, ?] in a way. But we'd have to have another two hours for me to explain to you why and how.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, we'll stop there, and thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the question, and then-- Walter, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Helene, born Flieg. My mother's mother. This was taken some time in Schrimm, obviously before she died. I'm not quite sure how I got it. But it may have been from my cousin in South America, who is a very well-known photographer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was dead before you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, I didn't know her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Abraham Flieg, my mother's father, the man who had the men's store in Frem, in the province of Posen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea whatever. Obviously, I don't even know when the man died. I could look it up, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, can you tell me about this document, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is what we talked about, and it's a little bit funny. That is my father's document that he received, the Iron Cross, Second Class, for the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, will you tell you about this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the document that you received-- my father received, the Cross of Honor for front fighters, which the Nazis made a very great deal of. Unless-- but they realized too late that too many Jews got this. When they found out, they played it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, who is this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother, Regina Hartmann, nee, Flieg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was '38, so she would have been-- she was born in '92. She was about 46, give or take a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: 46, OK. OK, Walter, who is this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father, Hans Hartmann, [GERMAN], Hans Hartmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken just a few weeks, very short time before Kristallnacht. Late-ish in November, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was born, '88, so he would have been close to 50, wouldn't he? 49, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, who is this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, again, is my father. And this may be a unique, or at least a very rare photo. The difference in time between this photograph and the one we just saw is about oh, a month, if that. This was taken just after he returned from Buchenwald, after Kristallnacht. And even if you forget about the hair, I think you will see the enormous weight loss. And then there's something about the eyes, which I, myself, don't like to look at.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, tell me about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture which I call the clan. My South American cousin made one for every one member of the family. These are the sisters and brothers of my mother, including my mother, and all their spouses. This was taken in the middle '20s, sometime, maybe a little later than that, and shows the entire gang.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chemnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chemnitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Uncle Daniel's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- is your father in that picture? Where is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is way on our right. My mother's standing behind him. The man who owns the place, is-- in whose apartment it was taken, is sitting just to the left, I suppose, you might say a little bit to the left of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK. Walter, can you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a very unusual, perhaps unique document. It is, if you please, the death certificate for my father, whose ashes were sent to my mother from the Registry, Standesamt, Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother had this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She left papers with the people who she lived with. And they saved-- they found us and sent them to us after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the synagogue of Chemnitz, which was destroyed totally on Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Walter, who-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's myself on the left, and my brother on the right, not very long before we left for England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our home, in Chemnitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: In

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these are, quite clearly, the people on top of the wedding cake, modeled after my wife, on the right, Phyllis, and myself, in this very elegant hat, on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- when-- where was this taken and the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Philadelphia, on February the 26th, 1947. In other words, just about 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Walter, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, in a way, I suppose you might say, this is the answer to Hitler and his gang. On the left is our second daughter, Julie. The next lady is my wife, Phyllis. Then I peep out from the back. Then comes my son, Alexander. And on right is glamorpuss, my eldest daughter, Gail.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken a little bit more than a year ago, when our second grandson became Bar Mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London, Ontario.

speaker: interviewer
text: London, Ontario. OK. Walter, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Starting from left in front is Jeremy Orbach, number one of the second daughter. Then is Aaron Golden, the third son of the first daughter. And then here's Benjamin Golden, the second son of the first daughter. Behind them, the big one, is Joshua Golden, number one of Gail Golden, and number one of everybody. And on his shoulder is Ethan, the number two of Julie Orbach, Ethan Orbach. This was taken at the summer home of Gail's parents-in-law near South Haven, Michigan. And I think of this as a further response to Hitler. This is an interesting end to this afternoon's occupation. This is Phyllis, my wife, who made up for and repaired all the damage that we've been talking about. PHYLLIS (WIFE): And this is the one thing that I wanted most out of the whole world, was my Walter. Right. PHYLLIS (WIFE): And we've been 50 years together. The next 50 years may be nice too, huh?
